title: Ken G. Hall

infobox.value: Kenneth George Hall  (1901-02-22)22 February 1901  [Paddington, New South Wales](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales"), Australia[[1]](#cite_note-ASO-1)
* Film producer * film director * studio executive

[AO](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_Australia "Officer of the Order of Australia") [OBE](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire "Officer of the Order of the British Empire") (22 February 1901 – 8 February 1994) was an Australian film producer and director, considered one of the most important figures in the history of the [Australian film industry](/wiki/Australian_film_industry "Australian film industry"). He was the first Australian to win an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian film producer and director (1901–1994)

| Ken G. Hall  [AO](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_Australia "Officer of the Order of Australia") [OBE](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire "Officer of the Order of the British Empire") | |
| --- | --- |
| Hall in 1950 | |
| Born | Kenneth George Hall  (1901-02-22)22 February 1901  [Paddington, New South Wales](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales"), Australia[[1]](#cite_note-ASO-1) |
| Died | 8 February 1994(1994-02-08) (aged 92)  [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), New South Wales, Australia |
| Occupations | * Film producer * film director * studio executive |
| Years active | 1928-1957 |
| Spouse | Irene Addison  ​  ​ (m. 1925; died 1972)​ [[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2) |

**Kenneth George Hall** [AO](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_Australia "Officer of the Order of Australia") [OBE](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire "Officer of the Order of the British Empire") (22 February 1901 – 8 February 1994) was an Australian film producer and director, considered one of the most important figures in the history of the [Australian film industry](/wiki/Australian_film_industry "Australian film industry"). He was the first Australian to win an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award").

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

Kenneth George Hall[[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2) was born in [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales"), a suburb of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), New South Wales, in 1901, the third child of Charles and Florence Hall.[[1]](#cite_note-ASO-1) He was educated at [North Sydney Boys' High School](/wiki/North_Sydney_Boys%27_High_School "North Sydney Boys' High School").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

At age 15, with the help of his father, he gained a cadetship at the Sydney *[Evening News](/wiki/The_Evening_News_(Sydney) "The Evening News (Sydney)")*,[[1]](#cite_note-ASO-1) where he became friends with a young [Kenneth Slessor](/wiki/Kenneth_Slessor "Kenneth Slessor"), then a cadet for another paper.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Two years later, he became a publicist for Union Theatres, initially working as an assistant to [Gayne Dexter](/wiki/Gayne_Dexter "Gayne Dexter").[[1]](#cite_note-ASO-1) He had a six-month stint as manager for the Lyceum Theatre then returned to publicity, working his way up to national publicity director, "the highest post in film publicity in Australia" at that time.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1924, Hall joined the American distribution company [First National Pictures](/wiki/First_National_Pictures "First National Pictures") as a publicist, and visited Hollywood the following year.

## Directing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Directing career")]

### Early years: 1928–1930

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years: 1928–1930")]

Hall began making films in 1928 when at First National he was assigned to recut and shoot additional sequences for a German movie about the [Battle of Cocos](/wiki/Battle_of_Cocos "Battle of Cocos"), *[Our Emden](/wiki/Our_Emden "Our Emden")*. The resulting film, *[The Exploits of the Emden](/wiki/The_Exploits_of_the_Emden "The Exploits of the Emden")*, was a local hit.

Hall moved back to Union Theatres, running publicity for the State Theatre in Sydney, and working on the campaign against the proposed entertainment tax from [Stanley Bruce](/wiki/Stanley_Bruce "Stanley Bruce")'s government. He eventually became assistant to [Stuart F. Doyle](/wiki/Stuart_F._Doyle "Stuart F. Doyle"), managing director of the company.

### Formation of Cinesound: 1931–1934

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Formation of Cinesound: 1931–1934")]

Doyle established [Cinesound Productions](/wiki/Cinesound_Productions "Cinesound Productions") to make local films and assigned Hall to direct a number of shorts including *[Thar She Blows!](/wiki/Thar_She_Blows! "Thar She Blows!")* (1931), about the whaling industry, and *[That's Cricket](/wiki/That%27s_Cricket "That's Cricket")* (1931). He then gave Hall the job of directing a film adaptation of the popular play *[On Our Selection](/wiki/On_Our_Selection_(1912_play) "On Our Selection (1912 play)")*, adapted by [Bert Bailey](/wiki/Bert_Bailey "Bert Bailey") from the writings of [Steele Rudd](/wiki/Steele_Rudd "Steele Rudd") about the adventures of a fictional Australian farming family, the Rudds, and the perennial father-and-son duo, 'Dad and Dave'. Hall persuaded Bailey to reprise his stage performance as Dad Rudd. The result was a [massively popular film](/wiki/On_Our_Selection_(1932_film) "On Our Selection (1932 film)"), which was among the top four most popular films in Australian cinemas in 1932,[[6]](#cite_note-6) earning £46,000 in Australia and New Zealand by the end of 1933.[[7]](#cite_note-Pike-7)[[8]](#cite_note-our-8)

Hall and Cinesound decided to follow it with another adaptation of a play written by Bailey, *[The Squatter's Daughter](/wiki/The_Squatter%27s_Daughter_(1933_film) "The Squatter's Daughter (1933 film)")* (1933). It was a melodrama set in the Australian bush, and starred a discovery of Hall's, [Jocelyn Howarth](/wiki/Jocelyn_Howarth "Jocelyn Howarth"), who later had a Hollywood career as "Constance Worth". It was successful at the box office. During this time he made a short documentary *[Ghosts of Port Arthur](/wiki/Ghosts_of_Port_Arthur "Ghosts of Port Arthur")* (1933).

Hall's third feature was *[The Silence of Dean Maitland](/wiki/The_Silence_of_Dean_Maitland "The Silence of Dean Maitland")* (1934), another melodrama based on a play (which had been based on a novel). It starred English stage star [John Longden](/wiki/John_Longden "John Longden"). The film encountered censorship issues but was highly popular.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-dean-10) *Dean Maitland* was released on a double bill with *[Cinesound Varieties](/wiki/Cinesound_Varieties "Cinesound Varieties")* (1934), a short film directed by Hall featuring several musical acts.

Hall's fourth feature, *[Strike Me Lucky](/wiki/Strike_Me_Lucky "Strike Me Lucky")* (1934), was a vehicle for stage comedian [Roy "Mo" Rene](/wiki/Roy_Rene "Roy Rene"), one of the most popular performers in Australia. It was Hall's first feature that was a film original, and not based on other material. However the film was not well received and Hall later said it was the only one of his movies not to go into profit.[[11]](#cite_note-strike-11)

### Middle career: 1935–1937

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle career: 1935–1937")]

Needing a sure-fire hit, Hall then persuaded Bert Bailey to reprise his role as Dad Rudd in *[Grandad Rudd](/wiki/Grandad_Rudd "Grandad Rudd")* (1935), based on a play co-written by Bailey. It was popular, although not as big a hit as *On Our Selection*.[[12]](#cite_note-grandad-12)

Hall intended to follow this movie with a version of *[Robbery Under Arms](/wiki/Robbery_Under_Arms "Robbery Under Arms")* but decided not to proceed because of uncertainty arising from a ban the NSW government had on films about bushrangers.[[13]](#cite_note-robbery-13) Cinesound ceased production for several months in 1935 to enable Hall to travel to Hollywood and research production methods.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Hall returned to Australia with new filmmaking equipment and an American screenwriter [Edmond Seward](/wiki/Edmond_Seward "Edmond Seward"), who was to take over Cinesound's story department. Seward wrote *[Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred_(film) "Thoroughbred (film)")* (1936), a horse racing drama based on the life of [Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap "Phar Lap"). It starred imported Hollywood actor [Helen Twelvetrees](/wiki/Helen_Twelvetrees "Helen Twelvetrees") and was a success.[[15]](#cite_note-helen-15)

Seward also wrote Hall's next film, *[Orphan of the Wilderness](/wiki/Orphan_of_the_Wilderness "Orphan of the Wilderness")* (1936), the story of a boxing kangaroo. It was meant to be a 50-minute movie in the vein of *Cinesound Varieties* but Hall decided to expand it to feature length.[[16]](#cite_note-orphan-16)

Hall returned to comedy with *[It Isn't Done](/wiki/It_Isn%27t_Done "It Isn't Done")* (1937), based on an idea by its star, [Cecil Kellaway](/wiki/Cecil_Kellaway "Cecil Kellaway"). It was the first movie Hall made from a script by Frank Harvey, who would write most of his subsequent films. It was also the film debut of [Shirley Ann Richards](/wiki/Shirley_Ann_Richards "Shirley Ann Richards").[[17]](#cite_note-done-17)

Richards was the female lead in Hall's next film, *[Tall Timbers](/wiki/Tall_Timbers_(film) "Tall Timbers (film)")* (1937), an adventure tale set in logging country, based on a story by [Frank Hurley](/wiki/Frank_Hurley "Frank Hurley").[[18]](#cite_note-tall-18) It was followed by *Lovers and Luggers*.[[19]](#cite_note-lovers-19)

### Comic series: 1938–1940

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Comic series: 1938–1940")]

Hall made two films with comic [George Wallace](/wiki/George_Wallace_(Australian_comedian) "George Wallace (Australian comedian)"): *[Let George Do It](/wiki/Let_George_Do_It_(1938_film) "Let George Do It (1938 film)")* (1938) and *[Gone to the Dogs](/wiki/Gone_to_the_Dogs_(1939_film) "Gone to the Dogs (1939 film)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-let-20) In between these two, he made a third Rudd film, *[Dad and Dave Come to Town](/wiki/Dad_and_Dave_Come_to_Town "Dad and Dave Come to Town")* (1938), which featured a performance from a young [Peter Finch](/wiki/Peter_Finch "Peter Finch"). This has been described as one of Hall's most personal movies.[[21]](#cite_note-town-21)

Hall gave Finch a bigger role in *[Mr Chedworth Steps Out](/wiki/Mr_Chedworth_Steps_Out "Mr Chedworth Steps Out")* (1939), a vehicle for [Cecil Kellaway](/wiki/Cecil_Kellaway "Cecil Kellaway").[[22]](#cite_note-ched-22) Hall produced and co-wrote, but did not direct, *[Come Up Smiling](/wiki/Come_Up_Smiling "Come Up Smiling")* (1939), a vehicle for [Will Mahoney](/wiki/Will_Mahoney "Will Mahoney").[[23]](#cite_note-come-23) He made a fourth and final Rudd, *[Dad Rudd, M.P.](/wiki/Dad_Rudd,_M.P. "Dad Rudd, M.P.")* (1940), which introduced [Grant Taylor](/wiki/Grant_Taylor_(actor) "Grant Taylor (actor)").[[24]](#cite_note-dad-24)

## World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War II")]

Film production at Cinesound ground to a halt with the advent of World War II, although Hall kept busy during this period producing and directing newsreels, documentaries and short subjects, including *[Road to Victory](/wiki/Road_to_Victory_(documentary) "Road to Victory (documentary)")* (1941) and *[Anzacs in Overalls](/wiki/Anzacs_in_Overalls "Anzacs in Overalls")* (1941).

Hall also did shorts with dramatised segments, such as *[Another Threshold](/wiki/Another_Threshold "Another Threshold")* (1942), and short features, *[100,000 Cobbers](/wiki/100,000_Cobbers "100,000 Cobbers")* (1942) and *[South West Pacific](/wiki/South_West_Pacific_(film) "South West Pacific (film)")* (1943).[[25]](#cite_note-cob-25)[[26]](#cite_note-south-26)

### *Kokoda Front Line* (1942)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Kokoda Front Line (1942)")]

His most notable newsreel was the Oscar-winning *[Kokoda Front Line!](/wiki/Kokoda_Front_Line! "Kokoda Front Line!")* (1942) – the first time an Australian film/documentary was awarded an Oscar.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Post-war career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-war career")]

### *Smithy* (1946)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Smithy (1946)")]

After the war Hall returned to feature film production, enjoying a big success with *[Smithy](/wiki/Smithy_(1946_film) "Smithy (1946 film)")*, a film biography of Australia's most famous aviator, [Sir Charles Kingsford Smith](/wiki/Charles_Kingsford_Smith "Charles Kingsford Smith"), which he produced, co-wrote and directed. This film was financed by [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures"), who went on to offer its star, [Ron Randell](/wiki/Ron_Randell "Ron Randell"), a long-term contract in Hollywood.[[29]](#cite_note-smithy-29)

However, attempts by Hall to make further feature films (particularly an adaptation of the novel *[Robbery Under Arms](/wiki/Robbery_Under_Arms "Robbery Under Arms")*, which he later described as "the film I wanted to make more than any other"[[30]](#cite_note-30)) were not successful, partly because the Greater Union cinema chain, who had backed all of Cinesound's films in the 1930s, were no longer enthusiastic about investing in local production. He was also stymied by the fact that the Australian government refused to allow money over a certain amount to be raised for films.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In particular, an attempt to raise £160,000 to make two films in collaboration with [Ealing Studios](/wiki/Ealing_Studios "Ealing Studios"), including a version of *Robbery Under Arms*, was refused government permission.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

His short subjects included *[Can John Braund Cure Cancer?](/wiki/Can_John_Braund_Cure_Cancer%3F "Can John Braund Cure Cancer?")* (1948), *[Fighting Blood](/wiki/Fighting_Blood_(1951_film) "Fighting Blood (1951 film)")* (1951), and *[Overland Adventure](/wiki/Overland_Adventure "Overland Adventure")* (1956).

His last documentary was *[The Kurnell Story](/wiki/The_Kurnell_Story "The Kurnell Story")* (1957).

## Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Television")]

In 1956, Hall became the first general manager for [Channel Nine](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") in Sydney, where he remained until 1966.[[33]](#cite_note-33) There he instigated the practice of showing feature films uncut; previously in Australia they had been cut to fit the television schedules.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Later years")]

On 1 January 1972, Hall was awarded the [Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") for his services to the "Australian motion picture industry."[[35]](#cite_note-35) The [Australian Film Institute](/wiki/Australian_Film_Institute "Australian Film Institute") recognised his ability to convey the unique Australian character on film, and his important contribution to the development of the Australian film industry, with a [Raymond Longford Award](/wiki/Australian_Film_Institute_Longford_Life_Achievement_Award "Australian Film Institute Longford Life Achievement Award") for "Lifetime Achievement" in 1976. In 1985, he was inducted into the [Logie Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_Logie_Hall_of_Fame_inductees "List of Logie Hall of Fame inductees"). He was a freemason.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Hall was vocal in his criticism of the [Australian New Wave](/wiki/Australian_New_Wave "Australian New Wave"), remarking in 1979, "the market for Australian films is flooded with mediocre to weak product. Too many of these films cannot stand up to the competition and will drown."[[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2) He supported the production of local commercial films, his motto being "Give the audience what they want."[[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2)

Hall suffered a [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") in 1993.[[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2) He died in Sydney on 8 February 1994.[[2]](#cite_note-obituary-2) He wrote an autobiography, *Directed by Ken G. Hall* (1977), later updated as *Australian Film: The Inside Story* (1980). His wife since 1925, Irene Addison, had died in 1972. Hall never remarried.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Legacy")]

In 1995, the [ScreenSound Australia](/wiki/ScreenSound_Australia "ScreenSound Australia") (later the National Film and Sound Archive) inaugurated the annual [Ken G Hall Film Preservation Award](/wiki/Ken_G_Hall_Film_Preservation_Award "Ken G Hall Film Preservation Award"), which was presented by NFSA each year to a person, organisation, or group that had made an outstanding contribution to Australian film preservation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Past winners of the award include [Paul Cox](/wiki/Paul_Cox_(director) "Paul Cox (director)"), [Phillip Noyce](/wiki/Phillip_Noyce "Phillip Noyce"), [Peter Weir](/wiki/Peter_Weir "Peter Weir"), producers [Joan Long](/wiki/Joan_Long "Joan Long") and [Anthony Buckley](/wiki/Anthony_Buckley "Anthony Buckley"), film historian [Judy Adamson](/w/index.php?title=Judy_Adamson&action=edit&redlink=1 "Judy Adamson (page does not exist)"), [Tom Nurse](/w/index.php?title=Tom_Nurse&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tom Nurse (page does not exist)") of Kodak Australasia, NFSA archivist and historian [Graham Shirley](/wiki/Graham_Shirley "Graham Shirley"), and [ethnographic filmmaker](/wiki/Ethnographic_film "Ethnographic film") [Ian Dunlop](/wiki/Ian_Dunlop_(filmmaker) "Ian Dunlop (filmmaker)") (2009).[[38]](#cite_note-ifmag2009-38)

Stage 3 at [Fox Studios](/wiki/Fox_Studios_Australia "Fox Studios Australia") in Sydney is named after him.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Filmography")]

### Feature films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ken_G._Hall&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Feature films")]

* *[The Exploits of the Emden](/wiki/The_Exploits_of_the_Emden "The Exploits of the Emden")* (1928)
* *[On Our Selection](/wiki/On_Our_Selection_(1932_film) "On Our Selection (1932 film)")* (1932)
